HMW-V04: Vendor Job List Redesign

How might we redesign the vendor's My Jobs table so that each service type shows the information that matters most to that vendor's workflow, instead of forcing all 5 services into the same generic columns?
The problem: The current table has 7 columns: Shipment | Customer | Service | Route | Pickup | Status | Cost. "Route" makes sense for FM Trucking (origin→destination) but is misleading for EC/CS/CR/OH (single location, not a route). Different services need different secondary info at a glance: FM needs driver+vehicle, EC needs MAWB, OH needs bag count+weight. The current design forces everyone into the same columns, so nobody gets exactly what they need.

Research: Uber Freight shows load-specific info per card (rate/mile, distance, deadhead). Flexport filters by mode/service type. Enterprise table UX recommends adaptive columns based on workflow context. The pattern: what you show should match what the user needs to decide.

Option A: Service-Aware "Context" Column

Minimal Change
Rename "Route" to a neutral column. Its content adapts per service type:
• FM: origin → destination + driver sub-line
• EC/CS: location + MAWB sub-line
• OH: location + bags/weight sub-line
• CR: location + bag count sub-line

Same 7 columns. Same table structure. Just smarter content in one column.
Pros
• Minimal code change, same table structure
• Each service gets its most important secondary info
• No disorientation when switching service filters
• Consistent column widths across all views
Cons
• Column header is vague ("Details"? "Where"? blank?)
• Still has Shipment + Customer + Service as separate columns, eating width
• Doesn't solve the deeper issue: the vendor doesn't think in "Shipment IDs"
Teleport OSVendor My JobsFleetHaleSun

Option B: Columns Change Per Service Filter

Maximal Info
When the vendor selects a service filter (FM, EC, OH, etc.), the table columns change to show service-relevant data. Default "All" view uses generic columns. Each service gets exactly the columns its operators need.

• FM selected: Customer | Route | Pickup | Driver | Vehicle | Status | Cost
• EC selected: Customer | Location | MAWB | Deadline | Status | Cost
• OH selected: Customer | Location | Bags | Weight | Status | Cost
• All/default: Customer | Service | Location/Route | When | Status | Cost
Pros
• Each service gets exactly the columns it needs
• Maximum information density per service
• FM dispatchers see driver + vehicle without squinting at sub-lines
• EC brokers see MAWB prominently
Cons
• Table structure changes when switching filters. Disorienting.
• More complex to build (5 different table schemas)
• "Where did the MAWB column go?" when switching from EC to All
• Breaks mental model of "the table is stable, the data changes"

Option C: Compact Two-Line Rows

Recommended
Rethink the column set from the vendor's perspective. The vendor doesn't think in "Shipment IDs." They think: "what service, for which customer, at what location, when, what's the status?"

New columns: Job (service+customer+ref) | Where + Context | When | Status | Cost

Each row has two lines: Line 1 is the primary data. Line 2 is the service-specific context:
• FM: route on line 1, driver + vehicle on line 2
• EC: location on line 1, MAWB on line 2
• OH: location on line 1, bag count + weight on line 2
• CR: location on line 1, bag count on line 2

The "Job" column merges Service tag + Customer + Shipment ref into one cell (service tag on left, customer bold, shipment mono below). This saves two columns worth of width.
Pros
• 5 columns instead of 7. More breathing room, especially at 768px.
• Each service gets its context info naturally via sub-lines
• "Job" column answers "what is this?" in one glance (service + customer + ref)
• Stable table structure whether you're on All, FM, or EC filter
• "Where" column adapts content but keeps the same position and width
• Works well on tablet — fewer columns to fit
Cons
• Two-line rows are taller than single-line rows. ~25% fewer rows visible.
• "Job" column packs a lot (service tag, customer name, ref number)
• Breaks from admin Jobs page column pattern (different column set)

15:00		 Verified MYR 190

Recommendation: Option C — Compact Two-Line Rows

Option C is the strongest because it solves two problems at once: it gives each service its contextual info AND it reduces from 7 columns to 5, making the table work better at every viewport width.

The "Job" column (service tag + customer + ref) is a key insight. The vendor already knows their own company. They need to know: what type of work (service tag), for whom (customer), and which shipment (ref). Packing this into one cell saves two columns worth of space that the old design wasted on Shipment and Service as separate columns.

The "Where" column is the real win. It shows the location/route on line 1 (the physical answer to "where am I going?") and the service-specific context on line 2 (driver for FM, MAWB for EC, bags for OH). Same column position, same width, always stable. The content adapts to what matters for that job type.

Option A is too timid. It fixes the column name but doesn't solve the width problem. Option B is powerful but disorienting, and complex to build. Option C hits the sweet spot: stable table structure, adaptive content, fewer columns, better tablet experience.

The 5-column set: Job | Where | When | Status | Cost — this is what the vendor actually needs to make decisions.
